quiz_questions.question_text: Which muscle type is under voluntary (conscious) control?

options.text: Cardiac muscle
Smooth muscle
Skeletal muscle
All three types are voluntary

quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle is the only muscle type controlled by the somatic (voluntary) nervous system, allowing conscious decisions to initiate or stop movement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cardiac muscle is found exclusively in the:

options.text: Walls of the intestines
Heart
Biceps and triceps
Walls of blood vessels

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle tissue is found only in the myocardium (heart wall), where it pumps blood continuously throughout a person's lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: Smooth muscle is located in the walls of which structures?

options.text: Bones and cartilage
Hollow organs such as the stomach, blood vessels, and bladder
The heart only
Skeletal attachments and joints

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle lines the walls of hollow visceral organs including the GI tract, blood vessels, urinary bladder, and airways, controlling involuntary functions like peristalsis and vasoconstriction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which two muscle types display visible striations under a microscope?

options.text: Smooth and cardiac
Smooth and skeletal
Skeletal and cardiac
None of the muscle types are striated

quiz_questions.explanation: Both skeletal and cardiac muscle cells contain highly organized sarcomeres with alternating actin and myosin filaments, creating the characteristic banded (striated) appearance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Intercalated discs are a unique structural feature of which muscle type?

options.text: Skeletal muscle
Cardiac muscle
Smooth muscle
All three muscle types

quiz_questions.explanation: Intercalated discs are specialized cell-to-cell junctions found only in cardiac muscle, containing gap junctions and desmosomes that allow synchronized contraction of the heart.

quiz_questions.question_text: Smooth muscle cells are best described as:

options.text: Long, cylindrical, and multinucleated
Branching with one or two central nuclei
Spindle-shaped (fusiform) with a single central nucleus
Flat and disc-shaped with no nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle cells are small, tapered at both ends (spindle-shaped), and each contains a single, centrally located nucleus with no visible striations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Skeletal muscle fibers are characterized by being:

options.text: Small and round with one nucleus
Long, cylindrical, and multinucleated
Branching with intercalated discs
Spindle-shaped and involuntary

quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle fibers are among the largest cells in the body, being very long and cylindrical with multiple peripherally located nuclei resulting from the fusion of many myoblasts during development.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which muscle type contracts most slowly but can sustain contractions for the longest duration?

options.text: Skeletal muscle
Cardiac muscle
Smooth muscle
All three contract at the same speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle contracts slowly but can maintain prolonged, tonic contractions with very little energy expenditure, which is essential for functions like maintaining blood vessel tone and bladder storage.

quiz_questions.question_text: Peristalsis in the gastrointestinal tract is carried out by:

options.text: Skeletal muscle
Cardiac muscle
Smooth muscle
Connective tissue

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle in the walls of the digestive tract produces rhythmic, wave-like contractions called peristalsis that propel food from the esophagus through the intestines.

quiz_questions.question_text: Muscle tissue accounts for approximately what percentage of total body mass?
quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle alone constitutes approximately 40% of body weight, and when cardiac and smooth muscle are included, muscle tissue represents about 40-50% of total body mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is autorhythmicity in the context of muscle tissue?

options.text: The ability of skeletal muscle to contract without fatigue
The ability of cardiac muscle cells to generate their own rhythmic electrical impulses without external nerve stimulation
The process of smooth muscle digesting food
The reflex contraction of any muscle when stretched

quiz_questions.explanation: Autorhythmicity (also called automaticity) refers to the intrinsic ability of cardiac pacemaker cells to spontaneously depolarize and generate action potentials, allowing the heart to beat without nervous system input.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'striated' mean when describing muscle tissue?

options.text: The muscle is involuntary and controlled by hormones
The muscle has a smooth, uniform appearance under the microscope
The muscle displays alternating light and dark bands due to the organized arrangement of myofilaments
The muscle is spindle-shaped and tapered at both ends

quiz_questions.explanation: The term 'striated' means having a striped or banded pattern, which results from the highly organized, repeating arrangement of actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments within sarcomeres.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is myology?

options.text: The study of nerve tissue
The study of bone tissue
The study of muscles and muscle tissue
The study of connective tissue

quiz_questions.explanation: Myology derives from the Greek root 'myo-' meaning muscle and '-logy' meaning study of, making it the branch of anatomy dedicated to the scientific study of muscles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'involuntary muscle' mean?

options.text: Muscle that can only contract during sleep
Muscle that operates without conscious control, regulated by the autonomic nervous system and hormones
Muscle that never contracts unless stimulated by a physician
Muscle that is found only in the limbs

quiz_questions.explanation: Involuntary muscle refers to muscle tissue that contracts without conscious thought, including cardiac muscle in the heart and smooth muscle in visceral organs, both regulated by the autonomic nervous system.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a gap junction in cardiac muscle?

options.text: A space between muscle fibers where nutrients are stored
A protein channel connecting adjacent cardiac cells that allows ions and electrical impulses to pass directly between them
The point where a nerve meets a muscle fiber
A gap in the sarcomere where no filaments exist

quiz_questions.explanation: Gap junctions are specialized protein channels found within intercalated discs that create direct cytoplasmic connections between adjacent cardiac cells, enabling rapid electrical signal transmission so the heart contracts as a coordinated unit.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'excitability' mean as a property of muscle tissue?

options.text: The ability of muscle to stretch without tearing
The ability of muscle to return to its original shape after being stretched
The ability of muscle to receive and respond to a stimulus by generating an electrical signal (action potential)
The ability of muscle to produce heat during rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Excitability (also called irritability or responsiveness) is the ability of muscle cells to respond to chemical or electrical stimuli by producing an action potential, which is the essential first step in triggering contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is contractility?

options.text: The ability of muscle to stretch when force is applied
The unique ability of muscle tissue to shorten and generate force when adequately stimulated
The ability of muscle to resist fatigue during prolonged activity
The ability of muscle to grow larger in response to exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Contractility is the defining property of muscle tissue, referring to its ability to actively shorten and generate pulling force, which no other tissue type can do.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'extensibility' mean in muscle physiology?

options.text: The ability of muscle to contract forcefully
The ability of muscle to be stretched beyond its resting length without being damaged
The ability of muscle to conduct electrical impulses
The ability of muscle to store calcium ions

quiz_questions.explanation: Extensibility refers to the capacity of muscle tissue to be stretched or extended beyond its normal resting length, which is important for allowing opposing muscles to lengthen during movement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is elasticity as a functional property of muscle?

options.text: The ability of muscle to shorten when stimulated
The ability of muscle to respond to nerve impulses
The ability of muscle to recoil and return to its original resting length after being stretched
The ability of muscle to grow new fibers after injury

quiz_questions.explanation: Elasticity is the ability of muscle to spring back to its original length after being stretched, much like a rubber band, which helps maintain normal muscle tone and readiness for contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'visceral muscle' mean?

options.text: Another name for skeletal muscle attached to bones
Another name for cardiac muscle in the heart
Another name for smooth muscle found in the walls of internal organs (viscera)
A type of connective tissue that supports muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: Visceral muscle is an alternative term for smooth muscle because it is predominantly found in the walls of visceral (internal) organs such as the stomach, intestines, bladder, and blood vessels.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient arrives at the emergency room with an irregular heartbeat. The cardiologist explains that the heart's pacemaker cells are misfiring. Which unique property of cardiac muscle allows these cells to initiate their own contractions without nervous system input?

options.text: Voluntary control via somatic neurons
Autorhythmicity, which allows cardiac cells to self-generate rhythmic electrical impulses
The multinucleated nature of the cardiac fibers
The spindle-shaped morphology of cardiac cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle possesses autorhythmicity, meaning specialized pacemaker cells in the sinoatrial node can spontaneously generate electrical impulses that set the rhythm of the heartbeat without requiring signals from the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is examining a tissue slide under the microscope and sees long, cylindrical cells with multiple nuclei pushed to the periphery and clear striations. Which type of muscle tissue is the student most likely observing?

options.text: Smooth muscle from the intestinal wall
Cardiac muscle from the heart
Skeletal muscle from a limb
Epithelial tissue from the skin

quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle is identified microscopically by its long, cylindrical fibers with multiple peripherally located nuclei and prominent cross-striations, distinguishing it from cardiac muscle (branching, central nuclei, intercalated discs) and smooth muscle (spindle-shaped, no striations).

quiz_questions.question_text: A runner experiences a painful cramp in her calf during a marathon. The cramp involves an involuntary sustained contraction of the gastrocnemius muscle. What type of muscle tissue is involved in this cramp?

options.text: Smooth muscle
Cardiac muscle
Skeletal muscle
A combination of smooth and cardiac muscle

quiz_questions.explanation: The gastrocnemius is a skeletal muscle in the calf, and cramps in this muscle represent involuntary sustained contractions of skeletal muscle tissue, often caused by dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, or muscle fatigue during prolonged exercise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with high blood pressure is prescribed a medication that relaxes the muscle in arterial walls to lower blood pressure. Which type of muscle tissue does this medication primarily target?

options.text: Skeletal muscle in the arms and legs
Cardiac muscle in the heart
Smooth muscle in the walls of blood vessels
All three muscle types equally

quiz_questions.explanation: Vascular smooth muscle in arterial walls controls vessel diameter; when smooth muscle relaxes, the vessel dilates (vasodilation), reducing peripheral resistance and lowering blood pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: During open-heart surgery, the surgeon temporarily stops the heart and later restarts it. The heart resumes beating on its own without being connected to any nerves. Which property of cardiac muscle explains this ability?

options.text: Voluntary control through somatic motor neurons
Autorhythmicity of cardiac pacemaker cells and electrical coupling through intercalated discs
The fusiform shape of cardiac muscle cells
The multinucleated nature of cardiac fibers

quiz_questions.explanation: The heart can restart independently because cardiac pacemaker cells possess autorhythmicity, and intercalated discs with gap junctions allow the electrical signal to spread rapidly through the myocardium, enabling coordinated contraction without external neural input.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology student observes that a donated heart continues to beat in a transport container before transplantation. The student is amazed that the heart beats without being attached to a body. Which characteristic of cardiac muscle best explains this observation?

options.text: Cardiac muscle is under voluntary control
Cardiac muscle has abundant mitochondria and autorhythmicity that allow it to beat independently of nervous system input
Cardiac muscle is actually skeletal muscle that has been trained to contract rhythmically
Cardiac muscle cannot fatigue because it lacks sarcomeres

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle's autorhythmicity means its pacemaker cells generate their own electrical impulses, and the abundant mitochondria (occupying about 25% of cell volume) provide continuous ATP, enabling the heart to beat outside the body as long as it has oxygen and nutrients.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pregnant woman is told that the smooth muscle of her uterus will contract during labor to deliver the baby. She asks why she cannot consciously control these contractions. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The uterus contains skeletal muscle that simply refuses to obey conscious commands
Smooth muscle is involuntary and is controlled by the autonomic nervous system and hormones like oxytocin, not by conscious thought
Labor contractions are actually caused by cardiac muscle within the uterine wall
She could control them if she practiced biofeedback techniques

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle is involuntary, meaning it operates under the control of the autonomic nervous system and hormones (such as oxytocin during labor) rather than conscious thought, which is why uterine contractions cannot be voluntarily started or stopped.

quiz_questions.question_text: A weightlifter can voluntarily flex his biceps to lift a barbell, but he cannot voluntarily speed up his heartbeat or stop his stomach from churning. Which classification of muscle control explains this difference?

options.text: The biceps, heart, and stomach all use the same type of neural control
Skeletal muscle (biceps) is voluntary and controlled by somatic motor neurons, while cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary and regulated by the autonomic nervous system
The heart and stomach are controlled by skeletal muscle that acts involuntarily
All three are voluntary, but the heart and stomach require special training to control

quiz_questions.explanation: The somatic nervous system provides voluntary control of skeletal muscles like the biceps, while the autonomic nervous system involuntarily regulates cardiac muscle (heart) and smooth muscle (stomach), which is why we can choose to lift weights but cannot consciously control heartbeat or digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elderly patient has been bedridden for several months after a hip fracture. The physical therapist notes significant wasting of the patient's leg muscles. Which property of skeletal muscle explains why these muscles shrank without use?

options.text: Skeletal muscle replaced itself with cardiac muscle during bed rest
Skeletal muscle requires regular stimulation and use to maintain its size; without it, fibers undergo disuse atrophy
Smooth muscle in the legs absorbed the skeletal muscle tissue
The bones dissolved the surrounding muscle tissue

quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle depends on regular neural stimulation and mechanical loading to maintain its mass; prolonged inactivity leads to disuse atrophy, where muscle fibers decrease in size and strength, which is why rehabilitation after immobilization is critical.

quiz_questions.question_text: A histology student is given three unlabeled tissue slides: one from the heart, one from the biceps, and one from the intestinal wall. She must identify each using only a microscope. Which set of features would correctly match all three slides?

options.text: Heart: spindle-shaped, no striations; Biceps: branching with intercalated discs; Intestine: cylindrical and multinucleated
Heart: branching cells with intercalated discs and central nuclei; Biceps: long cylindrical fibers with peripheral nuclei and striations; Intestine: spindle-shaped cells with no striations
All three slides would look identical under the microscope
Heart: multinucleated and cylindrical; Biceps: spindle-shaped with one nucleus; Intestine: branching with intercalated discs

quiz_questions.explanation: Each muscle type has distinct microscopic features: cardiac muscle shows branching cells with central nuclei and intercalated discs; skeletal muscle shows long, multinucleated cylindrical fibers with peripheral nuclei and prominent striations; and smooth muscle shows small, spindle-shaped cells with single central nuclei and no striations.
